field_summary: This field exhibits severe soil compaction, indicated by a high mean bulk density of 1.71 g/cm³, despite low Söhne vertical stress values. Vegetation health is moderately stressed, with a mean NDVI of 0.507 and 85.5% of the field below 0.70. Economic analysis indicates that mechanical tillage is not currently justified, falling into the 'marginal' ROI category.
risk_assessment: The entire field exhibits high-risk conditions primarily due to a severe mean bulk density of 1.71 g/cm³. This level of compaction significantly restricts root penetration, water infiltration, and nutrient uptake, even though the Söhne stress propagation values are low (mean 0.168 MPa, max 0.171 MPa). The moderate mean NDVI of 0.507, coupled with 85.5% of the field below 0.70 NDVI and a minimum NDVI of -0.294, strongly suggests that vegetation is experiencing stress directly linked to this physical soil impedance. The mean clay percentage of 36.2% indicates a soil texture that can benefit from organic matter for improved aggregation, but the high bulk density is the most immediate and severe limiting factor.

recommendations.notes: Based on the mean clay percentage of 36.2% (<40%), humic acid is prescribed to increase aggregate stability, water holding capacity, and CEC. Incorporate into the topsoil zone to a depth of approximately 15 cm to maximize interaction with existing organic matter and clay particles.

recommendations.implement_type: No-till drill or broadcast spreader with light incorporation
recommendations.cover_crop_species: Daikon Radish (5-8 lbs/acre), Cereal Rye (30-50 lbs/acre), Crimson Clover (10-15 lbs/acre)
recommendations.notes: Immediately after humic acid application, seed this cover crop cocktail. Daikon radish will provide biological drilling into the compacted profile. Cereal rye will stabilize topsoil and exude glomalin. Crimson clover will fix nitrogen and contribute to organic matter, enhancing long-term soil structure and compaction resistance.

Given the mean ROI of 1.009, which falls within the marginal range (0.8-1.2), deep mechanical tillage is not economically justified at this time. Focus on biological and chemical amendments to gradually improve soil structure.

Regularly monitor NDVI, soil bulk density, and subsequent crop performance. Reassess the economic viability of mechanical tillage in future seasons if bulk density remains critically high and projected ROI improves above 1.2.

economic_outlook: The mean Return on Investment (ROI) for mechanical intervention is 1.009, falling within the marginal range (0.8-1.2). This indicates that deep mechanical tillage is not economically viable for this field at present. Instead, a biological-only intervention (humic acid amendments and cover crops) is recommended. While these interventions have lower upfront costs, their impact on severe bulk density (1.71 g/cm³) will be slower than mechanical tillage. However, they offer a cost-effective strategy to improve soil structure, increase organic matter, and enhance nutrient cycling, potentially improving future cash crop yields and increasing ROI for subsequent seasons.
confidence_note: The prediction interval widths for ripper depth, with a mean of 11.3 cm and a maximum of 12.5 cm, indicate moderate uncertainty in precise compaction layer depth estimation, though this is less critical given the current recommendation to avoid mechanical tillage.
